topic: A person learns their childhood bully became a therapist
raw_output:
[story 1]

The scent of lavender and aged paper hung heavy in the air of Dr. Thorne’s office. Elias sat on the edge of the leather armchair, his hands twisting the hem of his sweater until the fabric bunched painfully against his ribs. He stared at the meticulously organized shelves, each book a silent judgment. He’d come here for a consultation about a persistent anxiety, but the silence of the room amplified the frantic drumming in his chest. When Dr. Thorne finally looked up, his gaze was startlingly familiar—the same sharp, assessing look that used to make Elias shrink into the shadows of the schoolyard. "So," Thorne began, his voice a low, measured baritone, "tell me what’s really bothering you today." Elias swallowed hard, the dryness in his throat making the simple act of speaking feel monumental. He looked down at his scuffed shoes, wishing the floor would swallow him whole.

[story 2]

I kept catching my reflection in the darkened windowpane, and I didn't recognize the stranger staring back. The sheer weight of expectation pressed down on my shoulders, making it hard to draw a full breath. Every email, every unexpected phone call, felt like a physical assault. I paced the length of the small apartment three times before stopping abruptly in front of the mirror, my knuckles white where I gripped the frame. Then, a voice cut through my spiraling thoughts—a voice that used to be the soundtrack to my worst days. "Stop moving," it commanded, smooth and utterly devoid of malice, yet possessing an undeniable authority. It was him. My former tormentor, now presiding over a room filled with soft lighting and quiet contemplation. I flinched, a tiny tremor running through my limbs, and immediately turned away, suddenly desperate to disappear.

[story 3]

The complexity of the new diagnosis spun in my mind like a broken carousel. It was too much information, too many interconnected threads pulling at my composure. I gripped the edge of the mahogany desk so tightly my fingers ached, the wood digging into my palms. My breathing became shallow, quick little catches in my chest. I wanted to retreat, to build a fortress of silence around myself, but the room felt too small, the air too thick with unspoken possibilities. Then, he entered. He didn't rush; he simply moved with an economy of motion that was unnerving. When his eyes met mine—those same assessing, knowing eyes—a strange, unsettling calm settled over the internal storm raging within me. I found myself unable to articulate a single coherent thought, just a knot tightening painfully beneath my sternum.
